candidateFor: Adults with musculoskeletal pain including neck, shoulder, lower back, or sports-related strains
People recovering conservatively from herniated discs or postural problems
Those experiencing chronic fatigue, poor sleep, stress, or digestive complaints
Patients who prefer a drug-free, non-surgical approach or want to complement conventional care
International visitors combining a wellness course with a Korea travel itinerary
Patients interested in Sasang constitutional medicine or a fully personalised treatment plan

whatToExpect.detail: The practitioner reviews your health history, checks your pulse and tongue, and examines posture or problem areas. They select which therapies to combine — acupuncture, Chuna manual therapy, cupping, moxibustion, or herbal medicine — and may classify your constitutional type if Sasang diagnosis is used.

Acupuncture needles are placed and left for roughly 15–20 minutes while you rest. Chuna manual therapy involves hands-on spinal and joint adjustment. Cupping or moxibustion may follow. If herbs are prescribed, a custom decoction is prepared for you to take over a set course. No anaesthesia is needed and you remain comfortable throughout.

whatToExpect.duration: No downtime; 1–2 weeks for a treatment course
whatToExpect.detail: You leave the clinic and resume normal activity the same day. Minor cupping marks or brief needle-site soreness fade within a day. For chronic complaints, practitioners usually recommend a series of sessions over one to two weeks. International patients typically plan a 1–2 week Korea stay to complete a recommended course.

whyKorea: Korean medicine is a licensed, university-trained profession integrated into the regulated national health system, with practitioners held to clinical standards rather than operating on the fringes of care.

Seoul districts including Gangnam, Myeongdong, and Insadong host foreigner-friendly clinics with English-speaking staff and transparent pricing, making access straightforward for international visitors.
Therapies including Chuna manual therapy and Sasang constitutional medicine are distinctive to the Korean tradition and not widely available in the same form outside the country.

A first consultation bundling acupuncture and adjunct therapies commonly totals approximately $100–$150, with herbal courses in the $200–$500 range — well below equivalent private integrative care costs in Western countries.

faqs.question: Is Korean medicine treatment painful?
faqs.answer: Most therapies are gentle. Acupuncture needles are very fine and typically cause only a brief prick or mild dull sensation; Chuna manual therapy uses controlled hands-on adjustments. Some people notice minor temporary soreness or small cupping marks afterward, both of which fade quickly and require no downtime or activity restrictions.

faqs.question: What conditions is Korean medicine commonly used for?
faqs.answer: It is most often used for musculoskeletal pain — neck, shoulder, lower back, frozen shoulder, herniated discs, and sports strains — as well as chronic fatigue, poor sleep, stress, and digestive complaints. It is a conservative, drug-free option and not a substitute for emergency or surgical care.

faqs.question: How much does Korean medicine cost in Korea?
faqs.answer: A single acupuncture session runs approximately $5–$15. A first visit bundling consultation, acupuncture, and adjunct therapies typically totals about $100–$150. Custom herbal decoction courses generally range from $200–$500 depending on ingredients and duration. Confirm the full plan and pricing with the clinic before you begin.

faqs.question: How many sessions will I need, and how long should I stay in Korea?
faqs.answer: For acute complaints a single visit may suffice, but practitioners typically recommend a series over one to two weeks for chronic conditions. International patients commonly plan a one-to-two-week stay. Ask the clinic at your first visit for a recommended session plan so you can schedule your time in Korea accordingly.

faqs.question: Can foreigners easily access Korean medicine clinics in Seoul?
faqs.answer: Yes. Clinics in Gangnam, Myeongdong, and Insadong are accustomed to international patients and often have English-speaking staff or English intake forms. Confirm language support and itemised pricing when you book. Walk-in appointments are sometimes possible, but booking ahead is recommended at foreigner-friendly clinics.
